  • We are thinking in a pub-sub system about one system that tells another system what to do, instead of the other system reacting to an event that happened.

The notification system in the example above is a bit too dumb for its own good, and as a result of that, the buyShares function has to compensate, and has to start doing part of the work of the notification system: notifying of significant events. But, if we follow the option of having multiple reducers for the same action, everything can become more clear:

function ordersReducer(state=initialState, action) {
  switch(action.type) {
    case "BUY_SHARES_REQUEST":
      // Add the request to the list of open orders
    case "BUY_SHARES_SUCCESSFUL":
      // Modify the list of open orders and mark the corresponding order as completed
    default:
      return state
  }
}

function notificationsReducer(state=initialState, action) {
  switch(action.type) {
    case "BUY_SHARES_SUCCESSFUL":
      // Add a notification indicating success
    case "TRADING_DAY_CLOSED":
      // Add a notification indicating no more operations possible
    case "OTHER_SIGNIFICANT_EVENT":
      // Notify of other significant event
    default:
      return state
  }
}

Now the notificationsReducer has the responsibility of figuring out what events are worth being notified, and buyShares only has to deal with buying shares and shouting to Redux that has started and succeeded at buying shares.

function buyShares(...) {
  dispatch({type: "BUY_SHARES_REQUEST", ticker, price, amount})
  return fetch(...)  // call API to buy
    .then(order => {
      dispatch({type: "BUY_SHARES_SUCCESSFUL", order.ticker, order.price, order.amount})
      // No more SHOW_NOTIFICATION, that's the notification's business, not ours.
    })
}

This change of paradigm may cause some trouble, as we now decouple the actions we dispatch from the effects they trigger. You may dispatch an action and see it causing changes in places you didn't want to change. So there is a tradeoff to be aware.

Assuming that Redux stores all the state in the app

You should be already limiting the state that your store manages, for example, ignoring minor details in forms. But, it is very tempting to assume that all the state worth of significance is in Redux. Therefore, we can do things such as serialize the state, save it to LocalStorage, send it over the network, then paste it back into Redux, and the app is back in the previous state. Well, at least, you should be making your state serializable, and not putting functions inside the state (which is too tempting as a FP advocate). But still, there are bits of the state that are not in Redux. Let's go to the buyShares example:

function buyShares(...) {
  dispatch({type: "BUY_SHARES_REQUEST", ticker, price, amount})
  return fetch(...)  // call API to buy
    .then(order => {
      dispatch({type: "BUY_SHARES_SUCCESSFUL", order.ticker, order.price, order.amount})
    })
}

So we have a promise that dispatches an action (and we can assume to modify the store) when it starts, and then dispatches another action (and modifies the store) when it completes. What happens if, between start and completion, we take the store, stop the app, ship the store to another machine, and restart the app there with the store? Well, the store will have noted that we ran BUY_SHARES_REQUESTED. But the promise that eventually would call BUY_SHARES_SUCCESSFUL didn't get shipped, and as a result, we will never call BUY_SHARES_SUCCESSFUL. So our app will have the order open forever.

Which means you have to think a bit deeper on using the store for restoring a specific state, especially if you want to support SSR where the server sends the state to the frontend and assumes it will work. This is especially bad with middlewares, as middlewares are likely to have even more effects and internal state, and that internal state will be related to Redux, but not inside the store. If you are not careful with this, refreshing your app (F5) on different pages or sections can have subtle bugs for your users to find, such as "the saga was never started, therefore the button dispatched the action, but no one listened or acted on it".

When dealing with SSR and reload, you have to implement an initialization protocol that resumes the app back in a working situation. Because this working situation is likely to be a small state set, you have to define operations and protocols to guide the state, so that all the corresponding operations and listeners get started, and the user can continue the operation, even if he has to redo part of it, such as filling again form data that was not saved.

item.title: Using Weak Electric Fields to Make Virus-Killing Face Masks
item.description:
Face masks help limit the spread of COVID-19 and are currently recommended by governments worldwide. 

Now, engineers at Indiana University demonstrate for the first time that a fabric generating a weak electric field can inactivate coronaviruses. The electroceutical fabric, described in a ChemRxiv preprint that has not yet been peer-reviewed, could be used to make face masks and other personal protective equipment (PPE), the authors say.

The fabric was tested against a pig respiratory coronavirus and a human coronavirus that causes the common cold. It has not yet been tested against SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19.

“The work is of interest for the scientific community; it will open new [areas to] search to provide smart solutions to overcome the COVID-19 pandemic,” says Mahmoud Al Ahmad, an electrical engineer at the University of United Arab Emirates, who was not involved in the research. While the concept will require more development before being applied to PPE, he says, “it is an excellent start in this direction.”

Beyond masks, the findings raise the possibility of using weak electrical fields to curb the spread of viruses in many ways, such as purifying air in common spaces or disinfecting operating room surfaces, says study author Chandan Sen, director of the Indiana Center for Regenerative Medicine and Engineering at Indiana University School of Medicine. “Coronavirus is not the first or last virus that is going to disrupt our lives,” he says. “We’re thinking about bigger and broader approaches to utilize weak electric fields against virus infectivity.”

Sen’s lab has been co-developing the electroceutical fabric technology, under the proprietary name V.Dox Technology, with Arizona-based company Vomaris for the past six years. Sen retains a financial stake in the company.

The technology consists of a matrix pattern of silver and zinc dots printed onto a material, such as polyester or cotton. The dots form a battery generating a weak electric field: When exposed to a conductive medium, like gel or sweat, electrons transfer from the zinc to the silver in a REDOX reaction, generating a potential difference of 0.5 volts. The technology is FDA-cleared and commercialized for wound care, where it has been shown to treat bacterial biofilm infections.

To be used in masks, moisture will need to be applied in some fashion. According to Sen, approaches could include embedding a hydrogel so it activates the dots or inserting liquid-filled piping on periphery of the mask. Moisture from exhaled air will continue to keep the fabric moist.

When the COVID-19 pandemic began, Sen and his team began to wonder if the technology might affect viruses as well as bacteria. Past work in the literature suggested coronaviruses rely on electrostatic forces for attachment and genome assembly, and Sen hoped an electric field would disrupt those forces and therefore kill the virus.

In collaboration with IU geneticist Kenneth Cornetta, who performed some of the initial virus experiments in his laboratory, the team exposed a pig respiratory coronavirus to the electroceutical fabric for 1 or 5 minutes. After one minute, they found evidence that the virus particles had begun to destabilize and aggregate, becoming larger than before exposure. That suggests the weak electric field was causing “damaging structural alterations to the virions,” the authors write.

Next, the team tested the virus particles exposed to the fabric against cells in a dish. “The infectivity was gone,” says Sen.

The results indicate “promise for this strategy,” says Murugappan Muthukumar, a professor of polymer science and engineering at the University of Massachusetts, Amherst, who was not involved in the study. “The authors' hypothesis that the electrostatic forces within the virus particles and between the virus particles and the fabric are important is correct and is a very good idea.”

Still, Muthukumar notes, it is difficult to extrapolate how the electric field affects the viral genome, and more work needs to be done to investigate the effects observed in the paper.

Since publishing the preprint, the team also tested the fabric against human coronavirus 229E, a cause of upper respiratory tract infections, and gotten similar results, adds Sen.

The team has submitted the data to the FDA in the hopes of receiving Emergency Use Authorization to use the fabric in face masks. The technology could even be incorporated into the manufacturing of N95 masks or as an insert, says Sen.

Vomaris currently sells their wound-dressing kits for between $38 and $69 online. Sen says the technology is inexpensive to manufacture and could be used in PPE at a modest cost.

Independent of Vomaris, Sen’s laboratory is developing a tunable electroceutical called patterned electroceutical dressing, in which the field strength can be altered depending on need. The dressing has shown to be safe for patients with wounds, says Sen, and is currently in clinical testing. 

Advertisement

About the Human OS blog

IEEE Spectrum’s biomedical engineering blog, featuring the wearable sensors, big data analytics, and implanted devices that enable new ventures in personalized medicine.

item.title: What Is Classical Music? A Family Resemblance
item.description:
“What makes Classical Music ‘classical?'” questions the essence of musical genre. In order to answer this question, we need to establish a framework to discuss what a genre is. I am going to extend Ludwig Wittgenstein’s idea of “family resemblance” to discuss what the concept of genre might entail. Applying this to Classical music, I will discuss what attributes appear in the “family” of Classical music and how this ties in with other genres.

What defines Classical music?

It is useful to have terms such as “Classical” or “Jazz” or “Rock.”. Using these terms, we can quickly bring a musical style (and its social baggage) to the mind of another person. Asking if a friend wants to come to a Classical concert suggests a much different experience than a Rock concert. And there are certainly musical differences between these two genres that makes it helpful to distinguish between them. Not only can people recognize the social aspects but they can differentiate in order to discuss musical style.

However, any distinctions at the edges of musical genres become fuzzy. Is Brian Eno’s Music for Airports Classical music? Is it Pop music? Or what about Frank Zappa’s Civilization: Phaze III? By investigating these edge cases, we can look deeper into the question of defining genre. What attributes do Mozart and Frank Zappa share? And what differences are there? What is the relationship between Music for Airports and Lo-Fi Hip Hop? These questions seem to show that musical genres are less about definition and more about loose groupings of musical material based on spectra of musical and social attributes.

A Family Resemblance

Austrian philosopher Ludwig Wittgenstein coined the term “family resemblance” in the mid-1900s. He used this term to dig deeper into the problem of language, specifically asking “what is a ‘game?'”. From this question, he theorized that there is no single attribute that makes something a game (having fun, competing, interacting with others, etc.). Instead, just like observing a family, there is a collection of attributes that can put things in recognizable groups even if they do not all share one “essential” attribute.

For music, there are collections of attributes that sort works into genres. As mentioned above, this can be both in terms of musical style as well as social understanding. And because certain works have more “canonical” attributes for a genre, people can immediately categorize them. But other works have attributes that are more distributed between genres and so they become musical edge-cases.

What makes music Classical?

Western Classical music contains many attributes that contribute to an understanding of certain works as “Classical.” Some major ones that I see are as follows.

  • Focus on formal development of musical themes over time
  • Presentation of works in a concert hall (with accompanying accoutrements)
  • Usage of symphonic instruments (and traditional vocal timbre)
  • Utilization of written music rather than learning/playing “by ear”
  • Drawing from others in the “Classical” tradition

Formal Development in Classical Music

For example, Beethoven’s Fifth Symphony starts with a recognizable theme (BUH-BUH-BUH BUMMM) and works with that musical material for the rest of the movement. While popular music may have foundational musical material (chorus, riff, hook, etc.), the focus is not on formal development. Instead, a pop song may repeat the same catchy phrase (no development) a handful of times throughout the song. This is not to say that all Classical music develops thematic material. There are plenty of works, especially short-form works such as songs, that do not focus on developing thematic material. However, it is a major attribute of a lot of Classical music and not in Pop music.

Classical Concert Presentation

The presentation of works is more of a social attribute but is still primary to distinguishing the Classical genre. Most Classical works are presented in a concert hall. A traditional Classical concert entails dressing in a certain manner for both the performers and audience. It entails sitting quietly until the right time to applaud and then doing so. And it entails having a paper program explaining the pieces that will be performed and their order in the concert. It would probably be unsettling to have Pop music performed in this way, though it would certainly be possible. For the works at the edges of the Classical genre such as Frank Zappa or Prog Rock pieces, a Classical presentation or Rock presentation could cause a listener to perceive the work as one or the other genres.

Instruments in Classical Music

Another major attribute is the usage of Classical instruments versus Rock/Pop instruments. Traditionally, Classical musicians perform on a large variety of acoustic instruments: strings, woodwinds, etc. And Rock musicians perform with electric guitars, drum kit, etc. An artist like Sufjan Stevens draws much closer to the Classical genre than ACDC because of his extensive usage of Classical instruments throughout his works (see “Out of Egypt…” from his Illinois album). And while there are Classical works that include drum kit and electric guitar (see 2×5 by Steve Reich) or Pop works that include orchestral instruments, attributes of instrumentation can shift genre perception. Hearing a new work by a new artist in a concert setting performed on traditional Classical instruments (especially if the music focused on thematic development) could lead someone to tell their friend about a cool new Classical composer they just heard.

Use of Written Score

Combined with these previous attributes, an extremely important characteristic is the Classical emphasis on a written musical score. Classical musicians read music to learn pieces, analyze from written scores, and hold the written score as authoritative. This is contrasted with Pop musicians who learn by ear and may not even be able to read music. If anything is written, it may be lyrics, perhaps chords, and maybe melody but no specific instructions on specific notes to play. Where Classical music dictates every note and aspect from a written score, Pop music provides a structure that performers interpret by ear. This is one of the largest differentiators of Classical versus Pop music. And while you could learn a Classical work by ear or write down exact notes to a Pop song, the ramifications of having a written score or not provide a huge differentiation in the musical style itself.

Classical Tradition

However, there is still one major attribute left that can help people decide whether a work is Classical or not. Especially in Classical music, but also in other genres, musicians are drawing from a previous tradition. Composers often create Classical music in relation to similar music from the hundreds of years prior. Composing a symphony, a successful Classical composer would need to be aware of how the symphony functioned from Mozart to Shostakovich. If musicians are constantly drawing from this tradition of formal design, harmonic function (or serialism), and other social and historical data, people will receive their music as more Classical regardless of instrumentation or presentation.

Conclusion

Music has certain attributes. And the collections of these attributes weigh someone’s perception of a musical work as being connected to a certain genre. So while it is helpful to have genre terms in order to communicate, asking questions like “what defines Classical music” does not lead to a singular definition. Instead, just like Ludwig Wittgenstein discussed with defining the term “game,” there is a family resemblance that people can recognize. Some pieces of music exist at fuzzy edges of genres where they cannot be easily labelled with a single term. But other pieces are immediately discussed with a specific label. And to me, that is the essence of genre.

item.title: Show HN: To Listen to Any Article, Just Add Playthis.link to the URL
item.description:
I was tired of having to constantly scramble to find a podcast I want to listen to when going for a walk or just running on the treadmill. I, however, usually have an abundance of blog posts or articles around the web that I'd like to read. The problem is walking is not very conducive to reading. So I made this quick and dirty site that pretty much works anywhere, no app install, no copy paste, no nothing. Just add the domain playthis.link before any URL in your browser and hit enter. It will redirect to an audio file that automatically starts playing. You don't need to click a 2nd button to play the file either. It just starts reading the text right away. You can do this on your phone and just pick it up and go for a hike.

Example:

Amazing blog post: https://www.kalzumeus.com/2010/01/24/startup-seo/

Audio version: https://playthis.link/https://www.kalzumeus.com/2010/01/24/startup-seo/

Github repo: https://github.com/soheil/playthis.link

item.title: Show HN: Firefox extension to sum all your open YouTube tabs video time
item.description:
Release notes for 2.0
* fixed major performance issues
+ clicking the extension icon on the addressbar now recalculates the time if needed
+ opening and closing youtube tabs triggers the total time calculation
* now only the remaining time is calculated, so if you've already watched 2 minutes of a 5 minute video, the extension will show you have 3 minutes left
More extensions by Tomas Martty

item.title: Typographical Printing-Surfaces (1916)
item.description:
Comprehensive survey of all forms of relief printing type, plates, etc. and the methods of and machines for their production. The most exhaustive work in English on the subject of typecasting machinery.

Notes

Scanned at 600dpi greyscale. Reduced to an effective 300dpi and converted to JPEG for presentation here. This is not a beautiful presentation; in fact it is quite ugly. These are simply the raw scans with minimal, automatic, processing. However, it would have taken too long to do good manual editing of each of the 900 page images. It's better to have this information available in the present form than not at all.

In England the copyright status of this book is unknown. (Legros lived from 1865-1933, Grant was born in 1857 but the date of his death is unknown without further research. English copyright on this work either has expired or will expire 75 years from the date of Grant's death).
